retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment as Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: From 2011 to 2019, the large HPTN052, Partner 1 and 2, and Opposites Attract studies followed mixed-status MSM and heterosexual couples to assess HIV transmission rates with the partner living with HIV on ART. These showed 100% efficacy in preventing HIV transmission if ART is taken as prescribed and an undetectable viral load is achieved (defined as <200 copies of HIV-1 RNA/mL of plasma). [19] [20] Subsequently, combined studies across 2600 couple-years of follow-up and more than 126 000 sexual acts estimate the risk of genetically-linked HIV transmission per 100 couple-years of vaginal or anal intercourse without condoms or the use of PEP or PrEP by the HIV-negative partner is zero (CI, 0.00 to 0.14).[CDC. HIV Risk and Prevention Estimates. 2022] The HPTN052 study estimates TasP effectiveness to be 96% amongst heterosexuals, including periods when pills were not taken. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Florida HIV Safety for Florida Clinical Laboratory Personnel -- Clinical Significance -- Goals of HIV/AIDS Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there is no vaccine or cure for HIV, though clinical trials and studies continue to be performed. ART, when started early, has enabled people with HIV to live long lives with chronic infection. [2] The CDC aims to implement a 90-90-90 plan, where 90% of people infected with HIV have been diagnosed, 90% with diagnosed infection are on ART, and 90% of those on ART have decreased viral load. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Complications -- Adverse Events
retrieved_docs.content: The ATN 110 and 113 studies showed decreased bone density in young men taking TDF/FTC oral PrEP compared to placebo, with greater declines in those aged 15 to 19 than those aged 20 to 22. [22] [23] While declines resolved during the 48 weeks after the studies were completed, declines were persistent in younger men. [21] [CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-2 Infection -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goals of antiretroviral therapy (ART) in HIV-2 are similar to those for HIV-1: prevention of transmission, immune restoration, durable viral suppression, and a reduction in morbidity and mortality. [45] However, there are very few trials in HIV-2 to specifically address when ART should be started, what agents may work best, or which regimens should be used in the setting of virologic failure. [46] Most treatment data used to guide HIV-2 management has been extrapolated from the wealth of studies performed on patients with HIV-1. Despite the lack of clinical evidence, it is clear that several combination therapies used in HIV-1 are also very effective in treating those with HIV-2. [47] [48] [49]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Ibalizumab: None

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Choice of Medication
retrieved_docs.content: In 2021, the WHO additionally recommended the dapivirine vaginal ring for women at substantial risk of HIV infection as part of combination prevention approaches.[WHO. Consolidated HIV Guidelines. 2021] Versus placebo, African women at risk of HIV had a relative risk of HIV of 0.71, with an absolute risk reduction of -2.23% at 1.4 to 1.6 years. [52] The dapivirine vaginal ring is not approved by the Food and Drug Administration and is not available for use in the United States, but a 2022 randomized control trial of postmenopausal women in the United States showed high acceptability of the ring as a means of HIV prevention. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Almost 21 million lives have been saved with antiretroviral therapy worldwide. However, current prevention and treatment services miss or inadequately serve millions. Key indicators of the quality of HIV care for optimal patient and population health outcomes include the linkage of HIV-positive individuals to care, documentation of treatment by patients linked to care, and attainment of viral suppression among patients who are treated. For example, in the United States in 2021, 80% of patients with HIV were linked to care within 30 days of diagnosis, defined as at least 1 viral load or CD4+ test. However, of all people with an HIV diagnosis in 2021, only 54% were retained in care (defined as ≥2 viral loads or CD4+ tests ≥3 months apart in 2021), and only 66% were virally suppressed (defined as an HIV RNA level of <200 copies/mL).

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Research and Innovation
retrieved_docs.content: HIV preventive and therapeutic vaccine research has likewise been ongoing since the 1980s. To date, a successful vaccine has been elusive. A genetic basis for HIV acquisition has been identified, providing a novel potential for a possible cure. A case study reported in 2019 described an individual with HIV-1 who underwent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for Hodgkin lymphoma using cells from a donor with a homozygous mutation in the HIV coreceptor CCR5 (CCR5Δ32/Δ32) maintained HIV remission without the need for HIV therapy. [39] ART was stopped 16 months after transplantation, with HIV-1 remission maintained over an 18-month follow-up after cessation.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: This reduction of transmission has become a widespread use of antiretroviral therapy for HIV-positive individuals who are with an HIV-negative partner. The successes of antiretroviral therapy have reduced HIV to a chronic condition in many parts of the world as progression to AIDS has become rare. Studies have found that 3-drug therapy has led to a 60% to 80% decline in the rates of AIDs, hospitalization, and death. By 2030, the CDC plans to implement a 90-90-90 plan (90% HIV diagnosed, 90% on therapy, and 90% suppressed).

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA does not approve investigational HIV drugs. Investigational drugs include those used to treat or prevent HIV and vaccines to treat or prevent HIV. These drugs are only available in clinical trials. No vaccines exist yet; however, researchers are studying this possibility. [6] The following are recommended starting regimens for the majority of patients with HIV-1 (treatment-naive patients): Bictegravir, tenofovir alafenamide, and emtricitabine Dolutegravir (emtricitabine or lamivudine) and TAF (or TDF) [7]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment as Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: For both clinical and public health outcomes, HIV treatment with antiretroviral therapy as soon as possible and preferably within 7 days after diagnosis is recommended for all individuals with HIV, regardless of their CD4 count or the stage of the disease.[HHS. Adolescents-Adults ARV Guidelines. 2023][WHO. Global Health Sector HIV Strategies. 2022] Uptake and adherence remain suboptimal in the United States and worldwide. Many factors contribute to this, including lack of access to quality healthcare, poor linkage between testing and treatment, uneven knowledge gaps in the understanding of undetectable = untransmittable, personal factors, and the social context in which HIV infection occurs. [34] [22] [23] [24] For example, the CDC estimates that 20% of individuals under clinical care for HIV in 2015 did not achieve viral suppression, and 40% did not maintain it for more than 12 months. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Research and Innovation
retrieved_docs.content: Research and innovation are crucial to accelerating the impact of HIV prevention measures. Improved HIV diagnostic technologies and new ARTs have been and continue to be active areas of research since the HIV virus was first identified in 1984. As the world reaches toward the 95-95-95 goals, implementation research determining the best interventions for specific contexts or populations that continue to experience higher rates of HIV has become increasingly important. [22] Who delivers the intervention, eg, peer advocates, pastors, or nurses Where the intervention is delivered, eg, clinic, community event, or rural community Which theoretical change pathway is targetted, eg, self-image, altruism, or stigma How testing and treatment are best linked, eg, physician referral processes, health navigators Impacts on HIV treatment indicators, eg, viral suppression, the incidence of STIs, or retention in care

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Capsid Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: The phase III clinical trial of lenacapavir involving 72 patients showed no serious adverse effects (grade 3 or above), with only injection site reactions and gastrointestinal symptoms identified. However, given the small numbers involved, post-marketing reports should be reviewed. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Antibody tests can detect HIV within 23 to 90 days after exposure to HIV. They are the technology used for most rapid tests and the only Food and Drug Administration-approved HIV self-test. Antigen-antibody tests detect the viral p24 antigen and antibodies the immune system develops in response to it. They are commonly used for laboratory testing, detecting HIV in 18 to 24 days after exposure. A rapid fingerstick antigen-antibody test can detect the virus in 18 to 90 days. Nucleic acid tests detect the actual virus in the blood. They can detect HIV within 10 to 33 days after exposure. They are used as a confirmatory test after an antibody or antibody-antigen test or when someone has a recent exposure and a negative antibody or antibody-antigen test.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal HIV -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The Panel on Antiretroviral Therapy and Medical Management of Children Living with HIV strongly recommends the initiation of ART in all pediatrics with HIV. [44] There has been a significant 80% to 90% decrease in morbidity and mortality since the introduction of ART initiation in neonates. [45] [46] As confirmed by results from the CHER trial and other studies, there is a decrease in viral reservoirs, opportunistic infections, and disease progression to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome with early initiation of effective and early ART. [47] [48] [49] [50] [51] [52] Infants with any level of risk of exposure to HIV should be started on the appropriate ART within 6 hours of birth. The goals of treatment for HIV-exposed neonates include a reduction in morbidity and mortality, suppression of viral replication, facilitation of HIV remission, viral control, prevention of disease progression, maintenance of immunologic function, reduction of opportunistic infections, and prevention of drug resistance. [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Epoetin Alfa -- Indications -- Adverse effects of zidovudine
retrieved_docs.content: In a combined analysis of 4 randomized controlled trials, epoetin alfa improved anemia in patients with HIV treated with zidovudine and endogenous erythropoietin levels less than 500 IU/L. Epoetin alfa increased hematocrit by 4.6% from baseline and reduced the number of blood transfusions per patient compared to placebo (3.2 units versus 5.3 units, respectively). Although an elevated quality of life was recorded, it was not statistically significant. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1230
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations comprise specific antiretroviral regimens, including the three‑drug combination abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and the two‑drug combination abacavir / lamivudine. Both regimens are classified as members of the broader category of combination antiviral therapies used to manage HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1025
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated because of potential adverse interactions. Several antiretroviral agents have preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. In addition to prevention, cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed as therapeutic agents for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the Integrase Inhibitors class of systemic anti‑infectives, reflecting their shared mechanism of targeting the viral integrase enzyme. This network of transmission risk, drug contraindications, preventive agents, and treatment options underscores the importance of integrated management strategies for individuals at risk for or living with HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir / lamivudine is a combination antiviral medication that is classified as a member of the group “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is an antiretroviral medication that functions as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it acts as a protease inhibitor that blocks the activity of viral proteases essential for HIV replication, highlighting its therapeutic role in managing HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 512
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to behaviors such as shared needle use, which facilitates viral transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are employed to prevent or treat HIV infection. Delavirdine‑mesylate, a non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor, may be used for prophylaxis against HIV infection. Integrase inhibitors—including cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—belong to the same pharmacologic class of systematic anti‑infectives and are effective in both preventing viral integration and treating established HIV infection. Cabotegravir is specifically noted for its therapeutic role, while dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are recognized for their preventive potential. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate prophylactic and therapeutic strategies while avoiding contraindicated drugs such as methotrexate sodium in individuals living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 776
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations are classified as direct acting antivirals, meaning they target specific steps of the HIV replication cycle. Within this category, the fixed‑dose combination of abacavir / lamivudine is listed as a member, representing one of the therapeutic regimens used to treat HIV infection by providing synergistic antiviral activity and helping to prevent the development of drug resistance.
